title: The Dark Tower II: The Drawing of the Three - The Ran(King) of King - Part 10 (spoilers)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FNVB3Ndlrw8

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to The Ran(King) of King with 1987's The Dark Tower II: The Drawing of the Three — a book he initially disliked (disappointed it left the weird-western desert for New York) but now suspects may be his favourite of the series, and therefore possibly his favourite King book full stop. He reframes the Dark Tower as less a western than a theme park: the tower at the centre, each book a different "land."

The opening is a punch to the face — Roland wakes from a decade's sleep and immediately loses two fingers to lobster-monsters, maiming the hero in the first ten pages. From there, three beach doors let Roland body-leap into three New Yorkers he draws into his quest: Eddie Dean, a heroin-addicted "prisoner" who trades the needle for the tower; Odetta/Detta, a wheelchair-bound woman with split personalities fused into Susanna; and the Pusher, a sadistic killer with shocking connections to Susanna and to Jake. He raves about the relentless, expertly structured pacing — the first 160-page "Prisoner" section as perhaps the best King ever wrote — gritty sci-fi grindhouse that handles addiction, racism and violence.

It goes straight to number one, dethroning The Gunslinger.

----

Is The Drawing of the Three the series' peak for you — and does maiming the hero in chapter one signal King at his boldest?
